words.shortDefinition: The property of certain operations that can be applied multiple times without changing the result.
words.simpleDefinition: The property of an operation where doing it once and doing it many times produces the same outcome — safe to retry without unintended side effects.
words.longDefinition: In API design, an idempotent request is one where making the same call multiple times has the same effect as making it once. GET, PUT, and DELETE are typically idempotent, while POST is not.
words.techContext: We implemented idempotency keys for our 'charge-customer' endpoint to ensure that a network retry doesn't result in the user being billed twice.
words.exampleSentence: Using an idempotent API is crucial for building resilient systems that can safely retry failed network requests.
words.connector: Think of idempotency like pressing the elevator button repeatedly — after the first press the elevator is called; pressing ten more times changes nothing.

words.shortDefinition: The process of dividing a large dataset into discrete pages.
words.simpleDefinition: Splitting a large set of results into smaller pages, so the API returns a manageable chunk at a time rather than dumping everything at once.
words.longDefinition: Prevents overwhelming the client or server with too much data in a single request. Common strategies include 'offset-based' (limit and offset) and 'cursor-based' (using a pointer to the last item retrieved).
words.techContext: We switched from offset pagination to cursor-based pagination to handle our billion-row activity feed more efficiently.
words.exampleSentence: The API response included a 'next_page_url' to help the client handle pagination automatically.
words.etymology: from Latin pagina ('page, leaf of paper').
words.connector: Think of pagination like chapters in a book — rather than printing every page in one go, you turn to a chapter at a time, requesting the next when you're ready.

words.shortDefinition: Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State; a constraint of the REST application architecture.
words.simpleDefinition: A REST constraint where API responses include links to related actions, so a client can navigate the API entirely by following links rather than hard-coding URLs.
words.longDefinition: A principle where the API response provides links to other related resources and actions. This allows the client to navigate the API dynamically without having hardcoded URLs for every endpoint.
words.techContext: By following HATEOAS, our API response for a 'User' includes links to their 'Orders', 'Settings', and 'Profile' resources.
words.exampleSentence: HATEOAS makes an API truly self-discoverable by providing the client with all the possible next steps in the current context.
words.etymology: Acronym for Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State.
words.connector: Think of HATEOAS like a choose-your-own-adventure book — each page tells you which pages you can turn to next; you don't need to memorise the whole structure.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of managing changes to an API over time.
words.simpleDefinition: The practice of labelling different iterations of an API so existing clients can keep using the old version while new clients adopt new behaviour.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to introduce breaking changes without breaking existing clients. Common methods include URI versioning (/v1/), header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapp.v1+json), or query parameter versioning.
words.techContext: We released 'v2' of our API to support the new JSON schema, but we'll continue to support 'v1' for at least another year.
words.exampleSentence: API versioning is essential for maintaining backward compatibility while the product evolves.
words.connector: Think of API versioning like software editions — 'Word 2019' and 'Word 2023' can coexist; documents made for one don't break just because a newer version exists.

words.shortDefinition: The actual data sent in an HTTP request or response.
words.simpleDefinition: The actual data content of a request or response — the body of the message, as opposed to the headers or routing metadata.
words.longDefinition: Refers to the 'body' of the message, excluding the headers and metadata. In a REST API, the payload is typically formatted as JSON or XML.
words.techContext: I updated the request payload to include the new 'marketing_opt_in' boolean field.
words.exampleSentence: The API returned a 400 Bad Request because the payload exceeded the maximum size limit of 1MB.
words.etymology: from 'pay' + 'load', originally referring to the part of a vehicle's load that generates revenue.
words.connector: Think of a payload like the contents of a parcel — the box and label are the headers, but the payload is whatever's packed inside.

words.shortDefinition: Representational State Transfer; an architectural style for providing standards between computer systems.
words.simpleDefinition: An architectural style for APIs built on standard HTTP — using URLs to identify resources and HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to describe what to do with them.
words.longDefinition: Relies on a stateless, client-server, cacheable communications protocol — almost always HTTP. RESTful APIs use standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to manipulate resources identified by URLs.
words.techContext: We designed our system as a collection of RESTful services to ensure they are easy to integrate with multiple client types.
words.exampleSentence: REST has become the most widely used style for building web-based APIs due to its simplicity and alignment with the web's native architecture.
words.etymology: Acronym for Representational State Transfer. Coined by Roy Fielding.
words.connector: Think of REST like a filing system — each folder (URL) represents a resource, and you either look in it (GET), add to it (POST), replace it (PUT), or remove it (DELETE).

words.shortDefinition: A query language for APIs and a runtime for fulfilling those queries with your existing data.
words.simpleDefinition: A query language for APIs where clients specify exactly what data they need — no more, no less — in a single flexible request, instead of hitting multiple fixed endpoints.
words.longDefinition: Allows clients to request exactly the data they need and nothing more. This prevents 'over-fetching' (getting too much data) and 'under-fetching' (needing multiple requests for one view). Developed by Facebook.
words.techContext: We switched our mobile app to GraphQL so we could fetch the user's profile, posts, and comments in a single round-trip.
words.exampleSentence: Using GraphQL allowed our frontend developers to define the data requirements of their components without needing backend changes.
words.connector: Think of GraphQL like ordering at a build-your-own salad bar — you list precisely which ingredients you want; the chef doesn't decide for you and doesn't bring things you didn't ask for.

words.shortDefinition: A high-performance Remote Procedure Call (RPC) framework.
words.simpleDefinition: A high-performance API framework that uses Protocol Buffers to serialize data and HTTP/2 to transport it — favoured for fast, low-latency communication between services.
words.longDefinition: Uses Protocol Buffers (protobuf) as its interface definition language and message format. It runs over HTTP/2, allowing for features like streaming, header compression, and multiplexing. Excellent for internal microservice communication.
words.techContext: We used gRPC for communication between our internal services because it is much faster and more type-safe than REST.
words.exampleSentence: The gRPC service uses bi-directional streaming to provide real-time updates to the dashboard.
words.connector: Think of gRPC like a specialised intercom system between offices — faster and more structured than shouting down the corridor (REST), but requires everyone to have the same intercom installed.

words.shortDefinition: A standard for defining and documenting RESTful APIs.
words.simpleDefinition: A standard format for describing a REST API's endpoints, request shapes, and response shapes in a machine-readable file — the blueprint other tools can read to generate docs or clients.
words.longDefinition: Formerly known as Swagger. It uses a YAML or JSON file to describe the endpoints, request/reponse formats, and authentication methods of an API. This file can be used to generate documentation, client libraries, and mock servers.
words.techContext: I updated the OpenAPI specification to include the new error response codes for our payment service.
words.exampleSentence: By maintaining an accurate OpenAPI spec, we can automatically generate our developer portal and interactive API console.
words.connector: Think of OpenAPI like a building's architectural drawings — the actual building (the API) is what matters, but the drawings let contractors, inspectors, and clients all understand it without walking every room.

words.shortDefinition: The principle that each API request must contain all the information necessary to process it.
words.simpleDefinition: A design rule where the server remembers nothing about previous requests — every request must contain all the information needed to fulfil it.
words.longDefinition: The server does not store any information about the client session between requests. This makes the API much easier to scale, as any server in a cluster can handle any incoming request.
words.techContext: Our API's statelessness allows us to spin up dozens of new instances behind a load balancer without worrying about session affinity.
words.exampleSentence: Because our API is stateless, the client must include the authentication token in every single request.
words.connector: Think of statelessness like a vending machine — it doesn't remember who used it last; every press of a button must include the full selection and payment.

words.shortDefinition: A specific URL where an API can be accessed.
words.simpleDefinition: A specific URL at which an API receives requests for a particular resource or operation.
words.longDefinition: Represents a point of entry for a client to interact with a specific resource or functionality of an application. Each endpoint is typically associated with a specific HTTP method (e.g., GET /users).
words.techContext: The mobile app was trying to hit the wrong endpoint because I forgot to update the base URL in the staging environment.
words.exampleSentence: I added a new POST endpoint for users to submit feedback directly from the app.
words.connector: Think of an endpoint like a specific counter in a government office — one counter for passports, one for driving licences; you go to the right desk for the right service.

words.shortDefinition: Create, Read, Update, Delete; the four basic functions of persistent storage.
words.simpleDefinition: The four basic operations on stored data: Create, Read, Update, Delete — the building blocks of almost every data-driven application.
words.longDefinition: Most APIs are essentially wrappers around these four operations for various resources. In REST, these map to POST, GET, PUT/PATCH, and DELETE respectively.
words.techContext: I spent the morning implementing the CRUD operations for our new 'Project' resource.
words.exampleSentence: Our admin dashboard provides a simple CRUD interface for managing user accounts.
words.etymology: Acronym for Create, Read, Update, Delete.
words.connector: Think of CRUD like managing contacts in a phone — you add a contact (Create), look one up (Read), edit the number (Update), or delete the entry (Delete).

words.shortDefinition: Any object, data, or service that can be accessed via an API.
words.simpleDefinition: In REST, any named thing the API exposes — a user, an order, a photo — identified by a URL and manipulated with HTTP methods.
words.longDefinition: In RESTful architecture, resources are the primary actors. Each resource is identified by a unique URL (URI) and can be manipulated using standard HTTP verbs.
words.techContext: We decided to treat 'Invoices' as a separate resource from 'Orders' to make the API more modular.
words.exampleSentence: The API allows you to retrieve a collection of resources by sending a GET request to the pluralized path.
words.etymology: from Old French resourse ('a rising again, recovery').
words.connector: Think of a resource like a record in a filing cabinet — each file has a unique label (URL), and you can read, update, or bin it.

words.shortDefinition: Logic that executes in between the incoming request and the final route handler.
words.simpleDefinition: Code that sits in the request/response pipeline and runs before or after your main handler — handling tasks like authentication, logging, or compression.
words.longDefinition: Commonly used for cross-cutting concerns like authentication, logging, request parsing, and error handling. Middleware can modify the request/response or block the request from proceeding if a condition isn't met.
words.techContext: We added a global middleware to our API to log the response time of every request to our monitoring system.
words.exampleSentence: The auth middleware rejected the request with a 401 because the API key was missing from the headers.
words.connector: Think of middleware like the security and check-in process at an airport — before you reach the gate (your handler), you pass through several checkpoints that each do one specific job.

words.shortDefinition: The ability to narrow down the results returned by an API based on certain criteria.
words.simpleDefinition: Allowing clients to narrow down a list of results by specifying conditions — return only users in London, or only orders above £50.
words.longDefinition: Usually implemented via query parameters (e.g., ?status=active). It allows clients to request only the subset of data they are interested in, reducing bandwidth and processing time.
words.techContext: I added filtering to the 'orders' endpoint so users can see only their orders from the last 30 days.
words.exampleSentence: The API supports complex filtering using a combination of multiple query parameters.
words.etymology: from Latin filtrum ('felt, piece of felt used for straining').
words.connector: Think of filtering like searching in a spreadsheet — instead of scrolling all 10,000 rows, you apply a condition and see only the rows that match.

words.shortDefinition: The ability to specify the order in which items are returned by an API.
words.simpleDefinition: Letting clients request results in a particular order — newest first, alphabetical, by price — so the API doesn't dictate a fixed sequence.
words.longDefinition: Typically implemented with query parameters like ?sort=created_at&order=desc. It helps users find the most relevant information quickly, especially in large datasets.
words.techContext: We updated the API to allow sorting by multiple fields simultaneously, such as 'price' and then 'popularity'.
words.exampleSentence: The default sorting for our product list is 'newest arrivals', but users can change it to 'lowest price'.
words.connector: Think of sorting like the column-header arrows in a spreadsheet — click 'Date' and the rows reorder; the data is the same, just presented differently.

words.shortDefinition: Allowing the client to request only specific fields from a resource.
words.simpleDefinition: Letting clients specify which fields they want in the response, rather than always receiving every field — reducing payload size and wasted bandwidth.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Sparse Fieldsets. It reduces the size of the response payload by excluding unnecessary data. This is particularly useful for mobile clients on slow networks.
words.techContext: We implemented field selection (?fields=id,name) to avoid sending large 'bio' and 'avatar' fields when only basic info is needed for a list view.
words.exampleSentence: Field selection helped us reduce our mobile app's average data usage by 30%.
words.connector: Think of field selection like a café menu where you tick only the sides you want — you don't get a default plate of everything; you get exactly what you ordered.

words.shortDefinition: The intentional slowing of a service to prevent abuse or system overload.
words.simpleDefinition: Deliberately slowing down or capping the rate of requests a client can make, to protect the service and ensure fair use among all callers.
words.longDefinition: While rate limiting might block a user completely, throttling gradually slows down their requests or limits their concurrent connections to ensure the system remains stable for all users.
words.techContext: The API started throttling our background worker because it was making too many simultaneous database connections.
words.exampleSentence: We use throttling to ensure that a single 'noisy neighbor' on our multi-tenant platform doesn't affect the performance for other customers.
words.etymology: from 'throttle' (to control the flow of fuel).
words.connector: Think of throttling like a water-flow restrictor on a tap — it doesn't shut the water off, but it ensures no single person drains the tank too quickly.

words.shortDefinition: A server that acts as an API front-end, receiving API requests and directing them to various services.
words.simpleDefinition: A single entry point that sits in front of multiple backend services, routing incoming requests, enforcing auth and rate limits, and aggregating results.
words.longDefinition: It provides a single entry point for all clients and handles common tasks like authentication, routing, rate limiting, and analytics. It abstracts the complexity of the internal microservices architecture.
words.techContext: Our API Gateway routes traffic to either the 'User Service' or the 'Catalog Service' based on the request path.
words.exampleSentence: We moved our authentication logic into the API Gateway so we don't have to implement it in every individual microservice.
words.connector: Think of an API gateway like the front desk of a large hospital — all visitors check in there first, get directed to the right department, and security is checked before anyone goes further.

words.shortDefinition: A suite of tools for API development that use the OpenAPI Specification.
words.simpleDefinition: A toolset built around the OpenAPI specification — it provides a visual interface to explore and test an API's endpoints directly in a browser.
words.longDefinition: Includes Swagger UI (for interactive documentation), Swagger Editor, and Swagger Codegen. It is the most popular ecosystem for building and consuming OpenAPI-compliant APIs.
words.techContext: You can test the new endpoints directly in our Swagger UI without writing any code.
words.exampleSentence: I used Swagger to generate a TypeScript client for our backend API in seconds.
words.etymology: Invented name for the toolset, possibly implying confidence/style.
words.connector: Think of Swagger like an interactive showroom brochure — instead of just reading about a car's features, you can sit in it and test every button.

words.shortDefinition: A 'reverse API' where the server sends data to the client when an event occurs.
words.simpleDefinition: A way for a server to push notifications to your application the moment something happens, rather than your app repeatedly asking 'has anything changed?'
words.longDefinition: Allows for real-time integration without the need for the client to constantly 'poll' the server for updates. The client provides a URL, and the server sends an HTTP POST request to that URL when a specific event happens.
words.techContext: We set up a webhook to notify our Slack channel every time a new high-priority bug is reported in our system.
words.exampleSentence: The payment provider sent a webhook to our server to confirm that the customer's subscription was successfully renewed.
words.connector: Think of webhooks like a doorbell — instead of walking to the door every few minutes to check if someone's there, you wait for the bell to tell you when to answer.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP authentication scheme using security tokens called bearer tokens.
words.simpleDefinition: An authentication scheme where the client includes a token in the request header to prove identity — whoever holds the token is granted access.
words.longDefinition: The name 'Bearer' indicates that the person who 'bears' the token is authorized to use it. The token is sent in the 'Authorization' header of the request.
words.techContext: Our API uses Bearer Auth with JWTs; the token must be included as 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'.
words.exampleSentence: The request failed with a 403 error because the Bearer Auth token was valid but did not have the 'admin' scope.
words.connector: Think of bearer auth like a concert wristband — showing it gets you in; the venue doesn't check your ID again, they just verify the band is valid.

words.shortDefinition: The mechanism that makes it possible to serve different versions of a resource at the same URL.
words.simpleDefinition: The mechanism by which a client tells a server what data format it prefers (JSON, XML, etc.) and the server responds in the best matching format it supports.
words.longDefinition: The client specifies what format they want (e.g., JSON or XML) using the 'Accept' header, and the server responds with the appropriate format. This allows the API to be flexible and forward-compatible.
words.techContext: Our API uses content negotiation to serve both standard JSON and a specialized binary format for our high-speed internal clients.
words.exampleSentence: The server returned a 406 Not Acceptable because the client requested an image format that we don't support.
words.connector: Think of content negotiation like ordering at a multilingual restaurant — you say which language you prefer, and the waiter responds in the closest match they can manage.

words.shortDefinition: The process of providing meaningful feedback when an API request fails.
words.simpleDefinition: The practice of returning meaningful HTTP status codes and descriptive error messages when something goes wrong, so clients know what happened and how to recover.
words.longDefinition: Good API error handling involves using the correct HTTP status codes (like 400 for bad input, 401 for unauthorized) and providing a structured JSON response body that explains exactly what went wrong and how to fix it.
words.techContext: I updated our global error handler to include an 'error_code' and a 'documentation_url' in every failing response.
words.exampleSentence: Clear error handling is what separates a frustrating API from a great one.
words.connector: Think of error handling like a good GPS — when it can't find your route it doesn't just go silent; it says 'road closed, recalculating' so you know what to do next.

words.shortDefinition: Unique identifiers used to authenticate a project or a user to an API.
words.simpleDefinition: Unique tokens issued to clients that they include with every request to prove who they are and to allow the server to track and limit their usage.
words.longDefinition: They are often used to track and control how the API is being used, such as preventing abuse or billing for usage. Unlike JWTs, they are usually long-lived and intended for machine-to-machine communication.
words.techContext: You can generate a new API key in the developer settings to start integrating your application with our platform.
words.exampleSentence: Never check your API keys into public source control; always use environment variables.
words.connector: Think of API keys like a library card — you show it each time you borrow something, so the library knows who's asking and can enforce limits if you exceed your quota.
